steps.text:
Welcome to Chess! 
 This tutorial will teach you the basics.
The goal of chess is to checkmate your opponent’s king — trap it so it cannot escape capture.
Chess is a two‑player strategy game played on a board with 64 squares.
The board has 64 squares arranged in an 8×8 grid, alternating light and dark colors.
Each player starts with 16 pieces on the back two ranks.
Please set up your board like this.

There are six types of pieces: pawn, knight, bishop, rook, queen, and king, each moving in a unique way. 
 In the following steps you will learn how.
Pawns move forward one square (or two squares on their first move) and capture diagonally. (e.g. pawn d2)
Knights move in an L‑shape: two squares one way and then one square perpendicular. Only the knight can jump over other pieces, all others can only move over unoccupied fields. (e.g. knight b1)
Bishops move diagonally any number of squares. (e.g. bishop c1)
Rooks move straight along ranks or files any number of squares. (e.g. rook a1)
The queen combines the power of a rook and a bishop, moving any number of squares straight or diagonal. (e.g. queen d1)
The king moves one square in any direction. (e.g. king e1)
Let’s learn how a pawn moves: move the pawn from d2 to d4.
Now move the knight from g1 to f3.
Practice capturing: capture the pawn on e5 with your pawn from d4.
Move e2 to e3
Move f1 to d3
Castling: move BOTH the king from e1 to g1 and the rook from h1 to f1.
En passant: when an opposing pawn moves two squares beside your pawn, you can capture it as if it moved one square. (e5 to d6)
Move d6 to d7

Promotion: move a pawn (d7) to the last rank and promote it to a queen.
 It can now move like a queen. Exchange your physical pawn with a queen.
Move f1 to e1
Move f3 to h4
Move d1 to f3
Check and checkmate: attack the opponent’s king so it cannot escape. (f3 to d5)
